query: open reduction vs closed reduction for condylar fractures

retrieved_docs.title: Nasal Fracture Reduction -- Technique or Treatment -- Closed Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: All nasal bone reduction patients should wear a dorsal splint for seven days. Not only does it help hold the bones in place, but it reminds the patient and others around them to be careful because the bones can quite easily displace again with minimal force. Most closed reductions do not require internal splints, but they may be useful in cases of comminuted fractures, septal dislocation, and inwardly collapsing nasal bones.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Lateral Humeral Condyle Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Interventions for Pediatric Lateral Condyle Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical management aims to reduce the articular surface anatomically. Surgical indications for these injuries include fracture displacement greater than 2 mm, articular surface incongruency, and loss of reduction on serial radiographs. A retrospective study reported that surgical delay more than 24 hours postinjury did not influence the clinical and functional results for pediatric lateral humeral condylar fractures with displacement greater than 2 mm. However, delayed open reduction and Kirschner-wire fixation may increase the incision length, probably due to increased edema. [27] Closed reduction with percutaneous fixation is a minimally invasive option, mainly for Weiss type 2 and Song stages 2 to 4 fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasal Fracture Reduction -- Technique or Treatment -- Open Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fractures that cannot be reduced adequately by closed techniques are candidates for reduction via open septorhinoplasty. [17] [18] Occasionally, the bony and cartilaginous injuries may be complex and require extensive manipulation, including grafting, osteotomies, and careful suturing in order to avoid leaving the patient with ongoing nasal breathing or aesthetic issues. The greater exposure and direct visualization afforded by an open rhinoplasty approach offer a major advantage over closed reduction for appropriate patients. When contemplating formal septorhinoplasty to correct traumatic deformities, waiting 3 to 6 months after the initial injury is advisable because it will allow enough time to resolve edema and the posttraumatic inflammatory state.

retrieved_docs.title: Nasal Fracture Reduction -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindications to a closed reduction of nasal bone fractures include: Severely comminuted fractures of the nasal bones and septum Open septal fractures A fracture that initially presents 2 to 3 weeks or longer after the initial injury Nasal bone fractures occurring with naso-orbital-ethmoid or Le Fort fractures that require open reduction

retrieved_docs.title: Talar Neck Fractures -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians almost exclusively use open reduction and internal fixation to treat displaced talar neck fractures. Specialists reserve nonoperative treatment for rare nondisplaced injury patterns or cases involving patients ineligible for surgery. Nonoperative treatment includes immobilization with a splint in the acute setting and transitioning into a short leg cast when the swelling has subsided. A spanning external fixator is often selected as a temporizing measure to stabilize the bony injury and allow observation of the soft tissue envelope. The goal of surgery is anatomic reduction, which can be challenging in cases involving high displacement, comminution, or angulation. A biomechanical study demonstrated that as little as 2 mm of malreduction of the articular surface alters contact stresses. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Distal Femur Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Open Reduction Internal Fixation
retrieved_docs.content: The 95-degree blade plate is a useful implant for fracture fixation of OTA 33 type A patterns, as well as for the stabilization of malunions and nonunions.  The placement of the blade plate is often a technically demanding procedure because three planes must simultaneously be corrected. Condylar malalignment may result if the chisel and plate are incorrectly inserted. Sliding barrel condylar plates are devices based on the compression screw used for hip fractures. These devices allow for the ability to apply compression across the femoral condyles. They allow better purchase in osteoporotic bone and only need two planes of correction when compared to the blade plate. Potential disadvantages with this construct include its bulky size and the requirement to remove a large amount of bone from the lateral femoral condyle. Due to its bulky size, the plate may cause symptomatic hardware and knee symptoms. Clinical studies have generally shown good results with fixed angled blade plate and sliding barrel condylar plate devices with 82% reporting satisfactory to excellent results at 9.5 years for condylar plate and 81% good or excellent results for the dynamic condylar screw. [20] [21] A recent study comparing the less invasive stabilization system (LISS) with minimally invasive sliding barrel condylar plates in the treatment of OTA 33 type A1 to C2 concluded that there was no advantage for locking plate designs. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Hip Dislocation -- Treatment / Management -- Indications for Open Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: A nonconcentric reduction (indicating a retained loose body or significant soft tissue injury preventing proper reduction) An associated acetabular or femoral head fracture that will require an open repair Femoral neck fracture A dislocation that is not reducible by closed reduction techniques Patients who do not warrant an open reduction should have an urgent closed reduction in the emergency department under procedural sedation.

retrieved_docs.title: Talar Neck Fractures -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Talar neck fractures typically undergo management with dual-incision open reduction and internal fixation techniques. While recommending the prompt reduction of fracture dislocations, one can delay definitive fixation until the soft tissue envelope is amenable without an increased risk of osteonecrosis. In a series of 25 patients with 26 displaced talar neck fractures with a mean follow-up of 74 months, Lindvall et al observed a union rate of 88% and concluded that a delay in surgical fixation does not affect the outcome, such as osteonecrosis or union. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Lateral Humeral Condyle Fractures -- Treatment / Management -- Surgical Interventions for Pediatric Lateral Condyle Fractures
retrieved_docs.content: Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) is recommended if minimally invasive procedures fail. A comparative study demonstrated that an anterolateral approach for open reduction of pediatric lateral humeral condylar fractures had better radiographic and clinical outcomes than a posterolateral approach and was thus suggested as the optimal technique. [33] ORIF is indicated for Weiss type 3 and Song stage 5 fractures. Lag screw osteosynthesis is recommended when performing an ORIF due to its lower rates of malreduction and subsequent complications. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Mandible Body Fracture -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There are two methods to treat mandibular body fractures, i.e., non-surgical conservative management and surgical management. Whether to treat the fracture using surgical or non-surgical means depends on the type, severity, and consequences of fracture. In non-surgical management, also known as closed reduction, maxillomandibular fixation (MMF) serves to stabilize the fracture. [9] It offers various advantages such as low cost as it does not require any surgical treatment and hospitalization, is a less invasive procedure, and low sensitivity to the professional experience. However, MMF should not occur in a non-compliant patient, patient with seizure disorders, severe pulmonary dysfunction, intellectual disability, psychosis, pregnancy, and multiple system injuries.  Indications for closed reduction include:

retrieved_docs.title: Nasal Fracture Reduction -- Technique or Treatment -- Closed Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: This is the most straightforward approach, with success rates of 60% to 90%; it is usually reserved for simple, noncomminuted fractures. [16] The fundamental principle is to apply a force opposite to the vector of trauma to achieve fracture reduction. Depressed segments of nasal bone can be reduced using an elevator. Alternatively, Walsham forceps can be inserted into the nasal cavity and rotated laterally to press the bones outward. Remember that sometimes the fracture line has to be widened first and then closed again afterward, especially if bones are overriding each other. Attention should be paid to the nasal septum, and where possible, the septal base should be repositioned into the vomerine groove as necessary. In the event that the patient is unable to undergo reduction within 10 to 14 days of injury, the bones may begin to set in an incorrect position; in these cases, osteotomies may be necessary to mobilize the bones, but osteotomies in and of themselves do not necessarily require an open approach to reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: Cervical Dislocation -- Treatment / Management -- Anterior open reduction and anterior cervical decompression and fusion:
retrieved_docs.content: These procedures are indicated when an MRI demonstrates cervical disk herniation with significant anterior spinal cord compression. Open reduction is performed using Caspar pins to distract vertebral bodies and add rotation. While effective for unilateral facet dislocations, it is less effective in reducing bilateral facet dislocations.

retrieved_docs.content: Alternatively, a posterolateral incision may be used by making an incision behind the supracondylar ridge to the lateral border of the ulna.

retrieved_docs.title: Talar Neck Fractures -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The uncommon nature of these injuries partially explains the lack of high-quality literature to drive evidence-based treatment strategies. Open reduction internal fixation is the predominant treatment for displaced talar neck fractures, with nonoperative approaches reserved for the uncommon nondisplaced injury pattern or cases involving patients ineligible for surgery. Classic complications following open fixation of talar neck fractures include osteonecrosis or avascular necrosis, posttraumatic arthritis, and malunion. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Radial Neck Fracture Repair In A Child -- Technique or Treatment -- Open Reduction
retrieved_docs.content: A lateral skin incision can be used by placing the elbow at 90 degrees and palpating the lateral condyle. Make a 5-6 cm gently curved skin incision over the lateral condyle.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 989
retrieved_docs.content: THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS is classified as a subcategory of treatment; fracture or dislocation of lower extremity (other than hip or femur). Within this category, a range of specific operative interventions are enumerated, including Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (e.g., genu varus or valgus); internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction; open reduction of fracture of femur; open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation; osteoplasty of femur; osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer; repair of malunion of femur distal to head and neck; revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure); and trochanterplasty. These procedures address various deformities, fractures, malunions, and prosthetic revisions of the thigh and leg, providing surgical options for correction, stabilization, and reconstruction. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical interventions within the broader context of lower‑extremity trauma and orthopedic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 219
retrieved_docs.content: The “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” category is a comprehensive treatment modality for fractures or dislocations of the hip and femur as well as for lower‑extremity injuries that do not involve the hip or femur. Within this framework a full spectrum of femoral procedures is organized as child entities, including growth‑modulating techniques such as “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus).” Direct operative interventions on the femur comprise “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur,” “Internal fixation of femur without fracture reduction,” “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur with internal fixation,” “Osteoplasty of femur,”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: reduction of closed greater tuberosity fracture** and **open reduction of closed humeral fracture with external skeletal fixation** are classified by the morphological descriptor **“كسر مغلق” (closed fracture)**. Additional closed‑fracture operations—open reduction of closed humeral fracture with internal skeletal fixation, closed radial shaft fracture, closed scapular fracture, closed ulnar shaft fracture, and closed clavicular fracture—are also child procedures of the same shoulder‑and‑arm repair category. A broader procedural concept, **internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction**, serves as a parent node for treatment groups across multiple anatomical regions (hip and femur, other lower‑extremity bones, radius and ulna). **Internal fixation of clavicle without fracture reduction** is a specific instance of this paradigm, connecting clavicular management to the same non‑reduction fixation strategy used for other long‑bone injuries. The taxonomy specifies allowed

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 950
retrieved_docs.content: Internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a specific surgical treatment used for managing fractures or dislocations of several long bones, including the hip and femur as well as the radius and ulna. It is classified as a particular form of internal fixation of a fracture (تثبيت داخلي للكسر), indicating that it belongs to the broader category of internal fixation procedures performed without prior fracture reduction. Additionally, this procedure is considered a subtype of internal fixation techniques applied to the clavicle without fracture reduction, reflecting its inclusion within the general family of bone‑specific internal fixation methods. Together, these relationships highlight that internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction is a targeted operative approach that fits within larger treatment categories for various skeletal injuries, emphasizing its role in orthopedic care for multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 877
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these statements highlight that both delayed sequelae across various skeletal regions and specific surgical interventions are directly linked to the underlying presence of a fracture, emphasizing the need for comprehensive assessment and management of fracture‑related pathology in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 209
retrieved_docs.content: The category “THIGH AND LEG: SURGICAL REPAIRS, CLOSURES AND RECONSTRUCTIONS” comprises a comprehensive set of operative interventions that target the femur and adjacent structures. Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate growth plates to correct deformities. Fracture‑stabilizing operations encompass “Femoral shortening with blade plate,” “Internal fixation of femur” (with and without fracture reduction), “Intramedullary nailing of femur,” “Open reduction of fracture of femur” (with or without internal fixation), and “Trochanterplasty.” Osteoplastic modifications are represented by “Osteoplasty of femur,” “Osteoplasty of femur, lengthening,” “Osteoplasty of femur, shortening,” and “Osteoplasty, femur; combined, lengthening and shortening with femoral segment transfer,” allowing alteration of femoral length and contour. Preventive and corrective measures include “Prophylactic treatment of femur,” “Repair of malunion, of femur, distal to head and neck,” and “Suture of periosteum of femur.” Prosthetic management is covered by “Replacement of prosthesis of femur with use of methyl methacrylate” and “Revision of total knee arthroplasty, all components (procedure).” Clinically, these procedures address the primary anatomical site of lower‑limb fractures, exemplified by the entity “fracture of lower limb” classified as an unspecified closed fracture of the femoral neck, underscoring the femur’s central role in diagnosis, fracture stabilization, growth modulation, length alteration, and implant revision within orthopedic care.

By connecting specific fracture entities, a comprehensive set of internal‑fixation and open‑reduction techniques, and hierarchical procedural categories to underlying bone‑density disorders, the knowledge base supports integrated treatment planning for orthopedic injuries of the hip, lower extremity, and shoulder‑arm region, while highlighting the importance of managing osteoporosis to reduce fracture risk and optimize surgical outcomes.

Child procedures include epiphyseal arrest techniques—“Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); combined distal femur, proximal tibia and fibula,” “Arrest, epiphyseal, any method (eg, epiphysiodesis); distal femur,” and “Arrest, hemiepiphyseal, distal femur or proximal tibia or fibula (eg, genu varus or valgus)”—which modulate or correct angular deformities during growth. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1010
retrieved_docs.content: The late effect of fracture of multiple bones, the late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones, the late effects of fracture of the lower extremities, the late effects of fracture of the upper extremities, and the late effects of fracture of the femoral neck are all described as being associated with a fracture (كسر). In addition, the open reduction of an open shoulder dislocation with fracture of the greater tuberosity is identified as a direct morphological manifestation of a fracture (كسر). Together, these concepts illustrate that both the various delayed sequelae of fractures in different anatomical regions and specific surgical procedures for fracture management are intrinsically linked to the underlying fracture pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The open reduction of closed greater tuberosity fracture, open reduction of closed humeral fracture with external skeletal fixation, open reduction of closed humeral fracture with internal skeletal fixation, open reduction of closed radial shaft fracture, open reduction of closed scapular fracture, juxta‑articular, and open reduction of closed ulnar shaft fracture are all surgical interventions that share the direct morphology of a closed fracture (كسر مغلق). Each procedure targets a specific site of an upper‑limb fracture, and the broader category of fracture of the upper limb is associated with this closed‑fracture morphology. Recognizing that these diverse open‑reduction techniques—whether employing external or internal skeletal fixation—are unified by the underlying closed fracture condition highlights the importance of precise fracture classification and appropriate surgical planning in orthopedic patient care.

Both internal fixation of bone without fracture reduction and internal fixation of fracture are members of the osteoporosis/low bone density SMQ, underscoring the relevance of bone‑weakening conditions to these interventions.
